items.title: Rewrite TPC3 in C++ with CMake and SFML
items.body:
Full port of the Python/pygame game to C++17, preserving all gameplay mechanics: pufferfish state machine, player physics, autotiling, sudden death, and tournament mode.

## Engine (`src/`)

- **`Controller`** – abstract bot base class with full Python API parity (`get_x/y`, `is_pixel_ground`, `get_tile_level_matrix`, `get_left/right_sudden_death`, etc.). Exposes `BotRegistry` singleton + `REGISTER_BOT` macro for static compile-time bot registration
- **`Level`** – `.txt` level parser with the same autotiling algorithm (17 tile variants)
- **`Player`** – walk/jump/gravity/collision physics, sprite animation with palette recoloring
- **`Fish`** – state machine using `enum class FishState {Rest, Right, Left, Falling, HeldA, HeldB}`; grab/kick/throw mechanics preserved exactly
- **`SuddenDeath`** – closing spike walls; `SUDDEN_DEATH_DELAY = 420` named constant
- **`ImageUtils`** – spritesheet chopping + three-color palette swap matching the Python `fixed_recolor` variants
- **`Game`** – SFML render loop, off-screen `RenderTexture` scaled with aspect-ratio preservation, 1/2/3 speed keys (`60/120/240 FPS`)

## Bots (`submissions/`)

Five example bots as header-only files: `example`, `example2`, `keyboard` (WASD), `keyboard2` (arrows/numpad), `pon_tu_nombre` template.

Adding a bot:
```cpp
// submissions/mybot.hpp
class MyBot : public Controller {
public:
    void info()     override { team_name = "Mi equipo"; look = 2; color = 1; }
    void behavior() override {
        if (get_x() > get_enemy_x()) go_left(); else go_right();
        if (is_sudden_death_active()) jump();
    }
};
REGISTER_BOT("mybot", MyBot)
```
Then `#include "submissions/mybot.hpp"` in `main.cpp` and rebuild.

## Entry point (`main.cpp`)

- `./TPC3 <bot1> <bot2> [--levels-dir <dir>] [--full-screen]` — single match across all levels in the directory
- `./TPC3 --tournament <config.json> [--auto]` — bracket tournament using nlohmann/json (vendored `third_party/json.hpp`); handles BYEs, third-place match, and tie resolution interactively

## Build

```bash
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
make -j$(nproc)
```
Requires `libsfml-dev` (SFML 2.5+) and a C++17 compiler.

items.title: Automatically convert main to C++ upon code generation
items.body:
Added scripts to convert code to C++ files and remove the `defaultTask` code.

The hooks for the scripts exist in the vcu.ioc file, and they are written using a polyglot script to work for both Windows and Mac (Needs to be tested for Mac). Since bash scripts do not need to have the extension `.sh`, but Windows needs `.bat` extensions, the files have been named `pre_generation.bat` and `post_generation.bat`. The better solution would have been to just call the Python scripts directly, but unfortunately the STM32CubeIDE does not support this. Therefore, this polyglot workaround has been devised.

The preprocessor script converts `main.cpp` to `main.c` files, then the code generates. This is to ensure any code written in `main.cpp` gets copied over into the newly generated `main.c`. The postprocessor script removes the `defaultTask` code and converts the `main.c` back to `main.cpp`.

Rewrite of #3 for cleanliness.

items.title: [main] Rewrite rootmkdir in C++
items.body:
# This Pull request:
Replace the python version of rootmkdir with a native one.

## Changes or fixes:
I changed one small thing from the original `rootmkdir`: calling:
```
rootmkdir foo.root:dir
```
when `foo.root` does not exist would create the file and the directory in the original, whereas in this version it will error out unless you pass the `-p` flag. The rationale is that the top-level file should count as a "parent directory", so we should not create it implicitly unless `-p` is specified. Calling `rootmkdir foo.root` works as before (except it will error out if the file already exists).

- [ ] updated the docs (if necessary)

items.title: Rewrite preprocess_corpus in C++ to eliminate full-file memory loads
items.body:
The Python preprocessor called `f.read()` on the entire corpus before splitting lines, consuming ~2× the file size in RAM (file bytes + `splitlines()` list). On a 14 GB corpus this means 28 GB+ just for I/O, before any n-gram counting.

## Changes

- **`preprocess_corpus.cpp`** — C++ rewrite implementing the identical two-pass algorithm:
  - Streams via `std::getline`; one line in RAM at a time regardless of corpus size
  - UTF-16 BOM detection preserved (full-file read only for that rare case)
  - Unicode-aware `isalpha` / `tolower` covering all Vietnamese code points (Latin Extended-A/B, U+1E00–U+1EFF)
  - Same pruning constants (`PRUNE_THRESHOLD=3M`, `PRUNE_KEEP=1M`) using `nth_element` for O(N) threshold selection
  - `~5×` lower per-entry counter memory (`unordered_map<string,uint32_t>` vs Python `Counter`)
  - 4 MB `pubsetbuf` on the output stream to reduce write syscall overhead

- **`Dockerfile`** (train stage) — adds compilation of the C++ source; `g++` is already present in that stage:
  ```dockerfile
  COPY preprocess_corpus.cpp preprocess_corpus.py train_lm.sh ./
  RUN g++ -O2 -std=c++17 -o preprocess_corpus preprocess_corpus.cpp
  ```

- **`train_lm.sh`** — switches the preprocessing call from `python preprocess_corpus.py` to `./preprocess_corpus`; `docker compose run --rm train bash train_lm.sh` continues to work unchanged.

items.title: Remove python3-mapnik from instructions for Debian 13
items.body:
It is used to test if Mapnik is installed. This can be achieved using `mapnik-render --version`, too.

Someone tried to follow the guide for Debian 13 on Kali Linux at Berlin Hack Weekend yesterday and failed because Kali Linux (based on Debian) does not package `python3-mapnik`. When I looked at their issue, I was surprised to see this package in the list and wondered why it is requiered at all. The sole purpose of Mapnik's Python bindings in this guide is testing the installation. The same can be achieved with `mapnik-render --version`. That is part of the `mapnik-utils` package and installed as instructed by the guide.

Python-Mapnik is a piece of software, I don't want to rely on. It does not have any releases. This and other reasons motivated me to port Nik4 to C++ and call it [Nik5](https://codeberg.org/Geofabrik/Nik5).

The Ukrainian translation needs an update after my change. Can I somehow mark it as outdated?

Should I apply this change to other guides, too?

items.title: Fix MAP navigation route dropouts and ACC index errors
items.body:
### Description
This PR addresses the stability issues reported in #511, improving the robustness of both the MAP and ACC plugins.

### Changes Made
1. **Adaptive Cruise Control (`Plugins/AdaptiveCruiseControl/main.py`)**:
   - Added length checks (`if len(point) >= 3`) before unpacking 3D coordinates in `get_vehicle_in_front`.
   - This prevents the `IndexError: list index out of range` that would crash the ACC radar thread when encountering malformed or 2D route points.
2. **MAP Navigation (`Plugins/Map/navigation/navigation.py`)**:
   - Implemented a fallback mechanism in `get_direction_for_route_start`. 
   - Instead of immediately failing and returning `""` (which drops the entire route) when a strict lane match isn't found, the system now gracefully falls back to the first available direction (`nav_nodes[0].direction`).
   - This keeps the navigation line stable through complex prefabs, roundabouts, and imperfect map nodes.

### Testing
- Tested in-game with both MAP and ACC enabled.
- Driven through complex prefabs and intersections that previously caused route dropouts.
- **Conducted 1,000 km of in-game testing, with no further stability issues observed in route planning.**
- **Result**: The navigation line remains stable, and the ACC plugin no longer crashes, significantly improving the user experience.

### Note for Future Refactoring
I understand the MAP plugin is slated for a full rewrite in C# later. These python fixes are intended as a temporary band-aid to significantly improve user experience and reliability in the meantime without requiring a major structural overhaul.

items.title: Add Python conversion of Qt C++ TagLister
items.body:
Converts `TagLister.h` / `TagLister.cpp` (Qt C++) to idiomatic Python as `convert/TagLister.py`. TagLister scans raw XHTML source and produces an ordered flat list of `TagInfo` records — one per tag in document order — used for structural navigation of XHTML content.

## Key translation decisions

- **`TagInfo` / `AttInfo`** — C++ nested structs become `@dataclass` types
- **Qt string types** — `QString`, `QStringView`, `QStringList` all collapse to `str` / `list[str]`; `QStringView::isNull()` → `is None`
- **`QList` ops** — `<<` / `takeLast()` / `removeLast()` / `last()` → `append()` / `pop()` / `[-1]`
- **`parseAttribute`** — C++ took `AttInfo&` by reference; Python version returns a new `AttInfo` instead
- **Private methods** — C++ `private:` block becomes `_`-prefixed methods
- **Error output** — `qDebug()` → `print(..., file=sys.stderr)`

## Usage

```python
from convert.TagLister import TagLister

lister = TagLister(xhtml_source)
for i in range(lister.size()):
    ti = lister.at(i)
    print(ti.tname, ti.ttype, ti.pos, ti.tpath)

# Attribute extraction
ainfo = TagLister.parseAttribute('<div class="foo">', "class")
# ainfo.avalue == "foo"
```

items.title: Port semantic analyser to C++ (Phase 4)
items.body:
## Summary

This PR completes Phase 4 of the C++ rewrite by porting the semantic analysis layer — the `Analyser` class and supporting infrastructure — from Python to C++. The analyser consumes `SegmentedCommand[]` and produces an `AnalysisResult` containing proc definitions, variable definitions, scopes, diagnostics, regex patterns, package tracking, command aliases, and unknown-handler analysis.

### Key Changes

**New Core Components:**
- `Analyser` class (split across three files for readability):
  - `analyser.cpp` — entry points and command iteration
  - `analyser_commands.cpp` — command-specific handlers (proc, namespace, foreach, for, switch, catch, try, if, while, dict, set, incr, variable, expr, interp)
  - `analyser_helpers.cpp` — variable tracking, scope management, naming, diagnostics, expression analysis
- `AnalysisResult` — complete semantic model with scope tree, proc/variable definitions, diagnostics
- `Scope` hierarchy — global, namespace, proc, and block scopes with proper ownership via `unique_ptr`
- Semantic type definitions: `ProcDef`, `VarDef`, `ParamDef`, `ProcArgTrait` (bitmask), `CommandInvocation`, `PackageRequire`, `RegexPattern`
- `StubParser` — parses inline `# tcl-lsp: stub` annotations for command signatures
- `ParamListParser` — parses Tcl proc parameter lists with default values

**Architectural Patterns (documented in `docs/cpp-rewrite.md`):**
- Ownership via `unique_ptr` trees (no manual `new`/`delete`)
- `std::optional<T>` for optional values (not bool+value pairs)
- `std::expected<T, E>` for error reporting with typed error enums
- Type-safe enums for diagnostic codes (`DiagCode`) and argument roles (`ArgRole`)
- Encapsulation with `friend` access for controlled mutation

**Diagnostic System:**

items.body:
Adds 1-body transition reduced density matrices (RDMs). The main changes are grouped below:

### New sparse RDM computation API

* Added a new module `api/sparse_rdms_api.cc` that exposes functions to compute 1-, 2-, 3-, and 4-electron RDMs (and all spin cases) between two `SparseState` objects, and integrated it into the build and Python interface (`CMakeLists.txt`, `forte2_api.cc`, `__init__.pyi`).

### CI sigma builder API enhancements

* Added methods to `CISigmaBuilder` for constructing a sparse state from a CI vector and for computing alpha, beta, and spin-free one-electron transition RDMs between CI vectors, with corresponding Python bindings and type hints.

### CI code refactoring and simplification

* Introduced a helper method `csf_C_to_det_C` to convert CI vectors from the CSF basis to the determinant basis, and refactored all relevant CI routines to use this helper instead of duplicating conversion logic. This affects all routines that build RDMs and cumulants from CI roots.

### Build system updates

* Registered new source files (`api/sparse_rdms_api.cc`, `ci/ci_sigma_builder_1trdm.cc`) in the build system to enable the new features.

These changes collectively expand the capabilities for RDM computations in the codebase and make the CI code more maintainable and easier to extend.

items.title: Rewrite voe-dl in C#/.NET 10 with Blazor Server frontend
items.body:
Ports the Python/Flask application to C#/.NET 10, replacing Flask with Blazor Server (SignalR-backed real-time UI) and BeautifulSoup with AngleSharp. The yt-dlp binary is retained for HLS/DASH manifest downloads; direct MP4 URLs are downloaded natively via `HttpClient`. After each successful download, TMDB metadata is resolved and a Jellyfin/Kodi-compatible `.nfo` sidecar file is written alongside the video.

## New project: `src/VoeDl.Web/`

- **`Services/DownloadService.cs`** — all 8 voe.sx source-extraction methods ported from `dl.py`: regex pattern matching, base64, ROT13, a168c, MKGMa, and obfuscated JSON deobfuscation. Uses `AngleSharp` for HTML parsing. Direct MP4 URLs are streamed to disk via `HttpClient` (no external binary required); HLS/DASH manifests are delegated to `yt-dlp`. On success, calls `TmdbService` to look up metadata and write the NFO sidecar.
- **`Services/TmdbService.cs`** — resolves movie and TV episode metadata from The Movie Database (TMDB) API using a Bearer-token access token. Parses scene-release filenames (e.g. `Peaky.Blinders.The.Immortal.Man.2026.GERMAN.720p.WEB`) into a clean title, year, and optional S×E marker. Calls `/search/movie` or `/search/tv` + episode detail endpoints, then writes a Kodi/Jellyfin-compatible `.nfo` XML file (`<movie>` or `<episodedetails>`) with title, plot, rating, genres, studios, TMDB/IMDB unique IDs, and poster artwork URL.
- **`Models/TmdbMetadata.cs`** — data model for movie and TV episode metadata returned by the TMDB API.
- **`Services/JobManagerService.cs`** — thread-safe in-memory job manager (pending → running → done/error/aborted) from `app.py`. Fires `JobsChanged` events consumed by Blazor for push-based UI refresh — no polling required.
- **`Components/Pages/Home.razor`** — replaces `templates/index.html`; same Bootstrap 5 layout, real-time job status via SignalR, duplicate-URL warning, abort/requeue actions.
- **`Program.cs`** — registers services, three named `HttpClient` instances (`"voe"` for page fetching; `"voe-download"` with `Timeout.InfiniteTimeSpan` for streaming large media files; `"tmdb"` with ****** for TMDB API calls), reads config from environment variables (same vars as before).

## Infrastructure

- **`VoeDl.slnx`** — solution file at the repo root (XML-based `.slnx` format introduced in .NET 10) referencing `src/VoeDl.Web/VoeDl.Web.csproj` under a `/src/` solution folder.
- **`Dockerfile`** — multi-stage .NET 10 build; runtime layer installs `python3 + pip + ffmpeg + yt-dlp` for HLS download execution.
- **`docker-compose.yml`** — adds `APP_VERSION` build arg passthrough; port mapping unchanged (`5000:5000`).
- **`legacy/`** — original `app.py`, `dl.py`, `version.py`, `requirements.txt`, `templates/` moved here for reference.

## Key design decisions

- Direct MP4 URLs (the common case on voe.sx) are downloaded entirely within the .NET process using `HttpClient` with `ResponseHeadersRead` streaming and per-10 MB progress logging — yt-dlp is not required on the host for these.
- yt-dlp is kept as a subprocess only for HLS/DASH manifests that require muxing — avoids a fragile wrapper layer and keeps the same battle-tested download path for those cases.
- `FindYtDlp()` honours a `YT_DLP_PATH` environment variable override, searches `%PATH%` using `PATHEXT` on Windows (finding `yt-dlp.exe`), and throws a descriptive `FileNotFoundException` if the binary cannot be located.
- TMDB lookups are opt-in: if `TheMovieDbApiAccessToken` is empty or absent the step is silently skipped and no NFO is written. The response language is configurable via `TheMovieDbLanguage` (default `en-US`).
- `JobManagerService` is registered as a singleton; `JobsChanged` event lets Blazor components subscribe without polling timers.
- All environment variables (`DOWNLOAD_DIR`, `DOWNLOAD_PATH`, `DOWNLOAD_TIMEOUT`, `CREATE_SUBFOLDER`) behave identically to the Python version.

## TMDB configuration

Add to `appsettings.Development.json` (already git-ignored) or set as environment variables:

```json
{
  "TheMovieDbApiAccessToken": "<TMDB API Read Access Token>",
  "TheMovieDbLanguage": "de-DE"
}
```

items.title: skill: import systematic-debugging with embedded instrumentation layer (#20)
items.body:
Closes #20

## Problem

Agents debugging embedded firmware bugs default to proposing software fixes before gathering hardware evidence. A stack overflow presenting as a corrupted pointer, an ISR timing violation presenting as garbled data, or a WDT reset presenting as a "random hang" all look like software bugs until hardware instrumentation rules them out. The generic superpowers `systematic-debugging` skill enforces the 4-phase root-cause discipline but has no embedded layer — it doesn't tell agents to reach for JTAG, RTT logging, or `uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark()` before proposing fixes.

Issue #20 asks for a verbatim import of the skill plus a new Phase 1.5 sub-section covering 7 hardware evidence-gathering techniques specific to embedded/FreeRTOS work.

## Prior PRs

Searched open and closed PRs for "systematic-debugging": one result — PR #70 (closed, not merged). PR #70 copied `condition-based-waiting-example.ts` verbatim from superpowers, did not address either amendment, and kept TypeScript examples in all sibling files. This PR supersedes it by fully applying both amendments: all code examples ported to embedded C / FreeRTOS, bare-metal patterns dropped (claudio is RTOS-only).

## Solution

### `plugins/common/skills/systematic-debugging/SKILL.md`

Copied from `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/SKILL.md` at SHA `917e5f5`, then adapted:

- Phase 1.5 — Hardware Evidence Gathering added between Phase 1 and Phase 2, covering all 7 required techniques:
  1. JTAG/SWD breakpoints/watchpoints — `openocd`/`pyocd` + `gdb`
  2. Logic analyzer/scope — SPI/I2C/UART capture vs. expected waveforms
  3. GPIO toggle instrumentation — scope-measurable ISR timing and critical section length
  4. `trice`/SEGGER RTT — non-blocking ISR-safe logging; reference to `plugins/common/rules/embedded/trice.md` (file exists at time of PR)
  5. `uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark()` — stack overflow investigation under load
  6. Link map — `arm-none-eabi-size`, `.map` file, `arm-none-eabi-nm --size-sort`
  7. Reset reason registers — `SCB->AIRCR` / RP2040 `VREG_AND_CHIP_RESET` read-before-clear
- `superpowers:test-driven-development` reference updated to `common:tdd`
- `superpowers:verification-before-completion` removed (no claudio equivalent)
- Word count: 509 words (9 over 500-word target, WARN zone, exits 0). Five rewrites exhausted against 7 mandatory Phase 1.5 techniques — as the issue amendment acknowledges, this skill legitimately needs a larger budget. The WARN exit-0 satisfies the acceptance criterion.

### Sibling reference files — ported to C

All three sibling files read verbatim from `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH` at SHA `917e5f5`, then adapted to remove all non-C code per amendment #1:

| Source file | Topic | Adaptation |
|---|---|---|
| `condition-based-waiting-example.ts` | FreeRTOS polling | Replaced with `condition-based-waiting-example.c` using `xQueueReceive` / `portMAX_DELAY`; bare-metal `__WFI` variant dropped (RTOS-only project) |
| `root-cause-tracing.md` | TypeScript instrumentation examples | Ported to C: `fprintf(stderr,...)` / SEGGER RTT, `rake test` instead of `npm test`, `dot` diagrams → `mermaid` |
| `defense-in-depth.md` | TypeScript validation layers | Ported to C: `assert()`, `#ifdef UNIT_TEST` guard instead of `process.env.NODE_ENV`, `mermaid` diagrams |
| `condition-based-waiting.md` | TypeScript `waitFor` generic | Replaced with FreeRTOS queue table and pattern description; bare-metal section removed; `dot` → `mermaid` |

### `find-polluter.sh`

Referenced by `root-cause-tracing.md` — adapted from the superpowers sibling (`npm test` → `rake test`, TypeScript glob → C test file pattern).

### `tests/prompts/common/trigger--systematic-debugging.txt`

Embedded failure prompt matching the scenario in the issue: FreeRTOS task hanging after ~2 minutes, heap fine, stack high-water marks low, no hardfault. Naming convention correct (`trigger--<skill-name>.txt`, `common/` subdirectory maps to `plugins/common`).

### `fix: clang-format Standard c++23 → Latest`

`clang-format` 22.1.3 (Homebrew) rejects `Standard: c++23` — the valid enum value is `Latest`, which resolves to C++23 in this version. Fixed as part of this PR since it blocked formatting `condition-based-waiting-example.c`.

## Known issue — `run.sh` needs `--verbose`

The trigger test (`trigger--systematic-debugging.txt`) passes when `claude -p` is invoked with `--verbose` — the skill IS triggered, as confirmed by inspecting the verbose log. Without `--verbose`, `stream-json` output does not include `tool_use` messages, so the grep checks in `run.sh` never find `"name":"Skill"`. This is a bug in `run.sh` (missing `--verbose` flag), not in the skill. To be fixed in a follow-up PR against `tests/run.sh`.

## Acceptance checklist

- [x] All files present in `plugins/common/skills/systematic-debugging/`
- [x] Iron Law `NO FIXES WITHOUT ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION FIRST` preserved verbatim
- [x] 4-phase skeleton with explicit ordering preserved
- [x] Red Flags section preserved
- [x] Rationalizations table preserved
- [x] "If 3+ fixes failed: question the architecture" gate preserved
- [x] Phase 1.5 added with all 7 hardware-evidence techniques
- [x] `description` frontmatter is trigger-only ("Use when a bug, crash, hang, test failure, or unexpected behavior needs investigation before proposing fixes")
- [x] Test prompt added; `run.sh` picks it up by naming convention
- [x] `trice.md` reference included (file exists)
- [x] No TypeScript, JavaScript, or Python code examples in any file
- [x] `condition-based-waiting-example.c` exists with FreeRTOS `xQueueReceive` conversion
- [x] `root-cause-tracing.md` and `defense-in-depth.md` contain only C examples
- [x] PR description lists every non-C example ported (table above)
- [x] `common:writing-skills-embedded` consulted before writing skill body (invoked via `Skill` tool in session)
- [x] `count_skill_words.py --budget 500` exits 0 (509 words, WARN zone; 5 iterations exhausted; Phase 1.5 budget justification above)

## Amendment conflict resolution

Amendment #1 said "keep the TS example as reference — polling patterns are language-agnostic". Amendment #1 (comment) superseded this: "Do NOT copy condition-based-waiting-example.ts verbatim." The comment takes priority — TypeScript file replaced with C.

Amendment #2 (second comment) added the `common:writing-skills-embedded` invocation requirement and word budget verification. Both satisfied.

The user clarified during implementation that claudio targets RTOS-only projects. The bare-metal `__WFI()` variant called out in amendment #1 as "ideally: a second variant" was dropped on this basis.

## Files read from `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH`

All read at SHA `917e5f5`:

- `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/SKILL.md`
- `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/root-cause-tracing.md`
- `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/defense-in-depth.md`
- `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/condition-based-waiting.md`
- `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/condition-based-waiting-example.ts`
- `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH/skills/systematic-debugging/find-polluter.sh`

I read each file listed above from the local `$SUPERPOWERS_REPO_PATH` — I did not guess, paraphrase, or fetch from elsewhere.

### Summary

This was split out from C++ work in #18885 as the review process kept pulling threads of things which were under-explained about managing memory when writing C or C++ code to work with MicroPython.

Goal of this PR:

* Give C and C++ programmers some pointers about managing memory in C code that interacts with MicroPython.
* Give C++ programmers some pointers about dynamic allocation & C++ standard library in MicroPython user modules.
* Re-arranged some of the docs around memory management and the `gc` module to reduce duplication but increase cross-reference linking.

(Pointer puns semi-intended. :grin:)

Some of the statements about C++ in this PR won't apply to all ports until #18885 is merged.

### Trade-offs and Alternatives

* I think the current "documentation" for this is reading the code, but that's prone to misunderstanding or not seeing all of the possible ways to solve a particular problem when porting C code to MicroPython.

### Generative AI

I did not use generative AI tools when creating this PR.

items.title: Python packaging
items.body:
## What this PR does

This PR adds a complete Python packaging story to the project:

1. **A self-contained installable wheel** — `pip install` / `uv sync` builds and
   installs the `mlir_ktdp` Python bindings + all native libraries. No separate
   MLIR installation required at runtime.

2. **A pure-Python utilities package** (`tools_ktdp`) — ships alongside
   `mlir_ktdp` in the wheel. Fully live-editable: changes to Python files take
   effect immediately without rebuilding.

3. **A `ktdp-walk` CLI tool** — inspect any `.mlir` file's operation tree from
   the command line.

4. **A pytest suite** — three parametrized fixtures covering dialect
   registration, flat KTDP ops, and nested control flow.

---

## How it installs

The build backend is [scikit-build-core](https://scikit-build-core.readthedocs.io/),
which drives cmake under the hood. `uv` manages the virtual environment and
locks all dependencies.

```
uv sync --extra mlir
```

That single command:
- Creates/updates the venv
- Installs `mlir_wheel` (provides MLIR cmake config + Python support libs)
- Runs cmake → builds the C++ extension + CAPI library
- Installs the project in **editable** mode

---

## Frozen dependency versions (`uv.lock`)

All versions are pinned in `uv.lock` for reproducible installs:

| Package | Pinned version | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `mlir-wheel` | `20260409+3b3ac5a11` | MLIR cmake config + bundled Python support libs |
| `nanobind` | `2.12.0` | Python ↔ C++ binding library (runtime dep) |
| `pytest` | `9.0.3` | Test runner (`--extra test`) |
| `lit` | `18.1.8` | LIT test runner (`--extra test`) |

`mlir_wheel` is an **optional** dep — users with a custom MLIR build can pass
`CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=..."` and skip it entirely.

---

## Wheel contents

The wheel is self-contained (~19 MB compressed, ~70 MB uncompressed). No
`mlir_wheel` needed at runtime.

```
ktir_mlir_frontend-0.1.0-cp312-cp312-linux_aarch64.whl
│
├── mlir_ktdp/                         ← cmake-managed
│   ├── __init__.py
│   ├── ir.py, passmanager.py, rewrite.py
│   ├── dialects/
│   │   ├── ktdp_nanobind.py           ← KTDP dialect Python bindings
│   │   ├── builtin.py, ext.py
│   │   └── _ktdp_ops_gen.py, _ods_common.py, ...
│   ├── extras/
│   │   └── meta.py, types.py
│   └── _mlir_libs/
│       ├── libKtdpPythonCAPI.so       34 MB  ← statically links all MLIR C API
│       ├── libMLIRPythonSupport.so     1.5 MB
│       ├── _mlir.cpython-312-*.so      0.3 MB ← core MLIR Python extension
│       ├── _ktdpDialectsNanobind-*.so  0.1 MB ← KTDP nanobind extension
│       └── libnanobind-mlir_ktdp.so   0.2 MB
│
└── tools_ktdp/                        ← scikit-build-core managed (pure Python)
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── __main__.py                    ← entry point for ktdp-walk
    └── ir_utils.py                   ← ktdp_context(), walk_module()
```

`libKtdpPythonCAPI.so` dominates size — it statically links all MLIR dialect
C API libraries so no other MLIR installation is needed at runtime.

---

## ktdp-walk demo

Parse any `.mlir` file and see a recursive, indented operation tree with result
types:

```
$ uv run ktdp-walk test/Ktdp/add-with-control-flow.mlir

builtin.module: []
  func.func: []
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    arith.constant: [index]
    ktdp.get_compute_tile_id: [index]
    arith.muli: [index]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    ktdp.construct_memory_view: [memref<96x64xf16>]
    scf.for: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      ktdp.load: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      tensor.empty: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
      linalg.add: [tensor<1x64xf16>]
        arith.addf: [f16]
        linalg.yield: []
      ktdp.construct_access_tile: [!ktdp.access_tile<1x64xindex>]
      ktdp.store: []
      scf.yield: []
    func.return: []
```

Indentation reflects nesting depth — `scf.for` body ops appear one level
deeper than the ops at the function's top level. Also usable as a library:

```python
from tools_ktdp import ktdp_context
from tools_ktdp.ir_utils import walk_module

with ktdp_context() as ctx:
    # ctx is an MLIR Context with all KTDP dialects registered
    ...

for op, depth in walk_module(source):
    print(f"{'  ' * depth}{op.name}")
```

---

## Tests

Four tests in `python/test/test_basic.py`, all passing:

```
$ uv run pytest python/test/ -v

platform linux -- Python 3.12.13, pytest-9.0.3
collected 4 items

test_basic.py::test_dialect_registration                PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[SIMPLE_MODULE]                 PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_BASIC_MODULE]             PASSED
test_basic.py::test_walk[KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE]      PASSED

4 passed in 0.02s
```

Each test fixture is an MLIR source string paired with an exact expected
`(op_name, depth)` list. A single parametrized `test_walk` asserts that
`walk_module` produces the complete expected traversal:

| Fixture | What it tests |
|---|---|
| `SIMPLE_MODULE` | Plain `arith` + `func` ops, flat structure |
| `KTDP_BASIC_MODULE` | `ktdp.construct_memory_view`, `ktdp.construct_access_tile`, `ktdp.load`, `ktdp.store` — all at depth 2 |
| `KTDP_CONTROL_FLOW_MODULE` | `scf.for` loop containing KTDP ops — verifies that `ktdp.load` and `ktdp.construct_access_tile` appear at depth 3, deeper than `scf.for` at depth 2 |

The depth check on the control flow fixture is the most important: it proves
the traversal is truly recursive and not just iterating the top-level ops.

---

## Developer workflows

### Recommended: `uv sync` with mlir_wheel

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --extra mlir --extra test   # build + install + test deps
uv run pytest python/test/          # run Python tests
uv run ktdp-walk path/to/file.mlir  # use the CLI tool
```

Edits to `tools_ktdp/` are live immediately. Edits to `mlir_ktdp/` or C++
require `uv sync` to rebuild.

### Custom MLIR build (no mlir_wheel)

```bash
uv venv --python 3.12
uv sync --no-install-project --extra test
CMAKE_ARGS="-DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir" uv pip install .
```

### Bare cmake — C++ only (no Python)

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
```

Python bindings are **off by default** — the cmake option
`KTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS` (default `OFF`) guards the entire Python setup
block. This prevents build failures on pure C++ environments where nanobind is
not installed.

### Bare cmake — with Python bindings

```bash
cmake -S . -B build -GNinja \
    -DMLIR_DIR=/path/to/llvm-build/lib/cmake/mlir \
    -DKTIR_ENABLE_PYTHON_BINDINGS=ON \
    -DPython3_EXECUTABLE=$(which python3) \
    -DLLVM_EXTERNAL_LIT=$(which lit)
cmake --build build -j$(nproc)
# then:
PYTHONPATH=build/python_packages/ktdp python ...
```

### LIT tests

```bash
cmake --build build --target check-ktir
# or directly:
uv run lit -sv build/test/Ktdp/    # must target build/, not test/
```

---

## Package architecture: why two packages?

`mlir_ktdp/` is entirely cmake-managed — cmake installs all its files
(generated Python, `.so` extensions, CAPI library) as part of the build.
Putting user-written Python in the same package breaks editable installs:
scikit-build-core's redirect mechanism only redirects files it discovers
itself, and it skips any file already installed by cmake.

`tools_ktdp/` is pure Python with no cmake involvement. scikit-build-core
manages it directly via `wheel.packages`, so editable installs redirect imports
to the source tree — edits are live with no rebuild.

items.title: Port Telos compiler from Python to C
items.body:
Full C implementation of the Telos compiler in `csrc/`, preserving all documented semantics: constraint-graph IR, polynomial closed-form detection, constant folding, x86-64 codegen, and C codegen.

### New files (`csrc/`)

- **`telos.h`** — All types: tokens, AST, IR, constraint graph, execution plans, public API
- **`lexer.c`** — Tokenizer (keywords, two-char operators, comments, float/int literals)
- **`parser.c`** — Recursive-descent parser, full expression precedence chain
- **`ir.c`** — IR node constructors, deep clone/free, algebraic simplifier, constant evaluator
- **`lifting.c`** — AST → constraint IR; accumulation loop → `ReductionConstraint` pattern detection
- **`optimizer.c`** — Polynomial decomposition, closed-form summation (degree ≤ 3), constant folding (≤10k iters), goal-graph liveness / dead-code elimination, cost-model plan selection
- **`codegen_x86.c`** — x86-64 machine code emission (System V AMD64 ABI, stack-slot allocation, jump patching)
- **`codegen_c.c`** — `long long`-based C source emission
- **`executable.c`** — `mmap(PROT_EXEC)` wrapper for native execution
- **`compiler.c`** — High-level API (`telos_compile`, `telos_run`, `telos_compile_c`) + CLI
- **`Makefile`** — `make` builds `telos`, `make test` runs suite
- **`tests/test_main.c`** — 45 tests across lexer, parser, lifting, optimizer, and end-to-end execution

### Verification

All optimizations from the spec are exercised end-to-end:

```bash
$ cd csrc && make test
=== Results: 45 passed, 0 failed, 45 total ===

$ ./telos ../examples/fixed_sum.telos
Function: fixed_sum  (29 bytes of x86-64 machine code)
  # constant-folded to mov rax, 45
```

Covers: Gauss formula (`Σi`), sum-of-squares (`Σi²`), sum-of-cubes (`Σi³`), linear combinations, constant folding, inclusive ranges, multi-function files, identity/arithmetic functions.

### Other changes

- **`.gitignore`** — Added `csrc/*.o`, `csrc/telos`, `csrc/tests/test_telos`
- **`README.md`** — Updated repo layout, added C build/run/test instructions alongside existing Python docs

Python implementation and its 119 tests are untouched and still pass.

items.title: Code Generator – Week 4 Exercise Andela Feb AI Engineering Bootcamp
items.body:
I'd like you to please review before I open the PR to upstream.

This PR adds my **Week 4 exercise**: a **Code Generator** that uses LLMs to convert Python to C++, add docstrings and comments, and generate unit tests. Uses **OpenAI** (GPT-4o-mini) and **Llama 3.2** (Ollama).

## What it does

- Let's users paste Python code and choose one of three actions: **Convert to C++**, **Add docstrings & comments**, **Write unit tests**
- Supports **model switch** (OpenAI, Llama via Ollama) for comparison
- Produces code-only output; strips markdown code fences when present
- **Gradio UI** for input, action, model selection, and output

## Implementation details

- **Gradio UI** with action dropdown, model radio, Python input textbox, and output textbox
- System and user prompts per action (C++ conversion, docstrings/comments, pytest unit tests)
- `generate_code` and `strip_code_block` for generation and output cleanup
- Same client setup as Week 2 & 3: OpenAI API key in `.env`, Ollama for Llama
- Lower temperature (0.3) for more stable code generation

## Business application

This is a practical prototype for code generation: translating Python to C++ for performance, documenting Python code, and creating tests. It shows how multiple models and prompt templates can support developer workflows and could be extended into an IDE extension or CI integration.

## Files changed

- `week4/community-contributions/winniekariuki/week4 EXERCISE.ipynb` — Code generator and Gradio UI
- `week4/community-contributions/winniekariuki/README.md` — Contents and how to run
